title: AFCA on track to hit record high complaints in FY26
full_text:
The Australian Financial Complaints Authority (AFCA) is on track for record high complaints in the 2025-26 financial year.
An update by the complaints authority found that it has received 95,571 complaints in FY26, as at 30 April, 10,545 of which came in April alone.
Based on the publicly available data, it has already seen four months in FY26 where complaints exceeded 10,000, while its lowest for this period was in December when it saw 8,252.
Of the total complaints, just 47,358 actually progressed through AFCA and $344.5 million has been paid out in compensation, including $36.3 million in April.
With another two months of data and complaints still to come through, AFCA is well on track to exceed 100,000 complaints in FY26 for the third consecutive year. FY25 currently holds the record for most complaints at 104,861, and the year prior saw 100,745.
The collapses of Shield and First Guardian have undoubtedly contributed to the higher number of complaints, with InterPrac Financial Planning – which has been deeply embroiled in the fallout from these funds – receiving 1,156.
This far exceeds any other financial advice business, though many of those with the highest complaints have also been tied to Shield or First Guardian in some way.
Advice firms with most FY26 complaints
Source: AFCA, June 2026
Taking a broader look, banks dominated the top five for complaints overall, with all four major banks represented, though they are also much larger businesses as well.
Most AFCA complaints in FY26
Source: AFCA, June 2026
Backlash related to these funds would not be the only contributor to inflated complaint rates, however, as AFCA has also been expanding its purview in recent times, allowing it to hear complaints from a broader array of sectors.
Backlash related to these funds would not be the only contributor to inflated complaint rates, however, as AFCA has also been expanding its purview in recent times, allowing it to hear complaints from a broader array of sectors.
Speaking at My Dealer Services’ conference in Sydney last week, AFCA senior ombudsman Nick Batard detailed the complaints process, pointing out that while some may feel they always side with the complainant, they often find in favour of the advice firm.
“AFCA is not out to hurt advisers. Our job is to be impartial, our job is to take each case on its merits and apply the AFCA rules, and I’ve had advisers, licensees, say, ‘Oh, you’re always fine in the complainant’s favour’.
“It’s really not true and excluding insolvent firms, for the last year – and these stats are hot off the presses – we find in a financial firm’s favour 65 per cent of the time, so nearly two-thirds of the time we’re finding against the complainant.”
author: Shy-Ann Arkinstall
heading: Regulation
page_title: AFCA on track to hit record high complaints in FY26 - Money Management
